text: The patient is positioned supine in a Trendelenburg position with an angle of 25 to 40 degrees, with arms placed alongside the body.
The Ravin GSI surgical system is positioned in a side-docked configuration with its third arm located on the patient's right side.
Laparoscopic assistance is provided from the left side using a 12mm and a 5mm trocar, visible in the surgical field.
Following the lysis of peritoneal adhesions, the surgeon identifies and dissects the ureters in their pelvic portions, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon sends the resection margins for pathological examination, indicating the completion of a tissue resection step.
The surgeon opens the pouch of Douglas and carefully dissects the posterior aspect of the bladder, taking precautions to avoid bleeding during this stage of the procedure.
The surgeon uses low-energy bipolar cautery to carefully dissect the seminal vesicles free, taking care to avoid damage to the surrounding neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon sections the omvius fascia and develops the posterior plane, revealing the prostatic apex.
The surgeon dissects the lateral walls of the bladder by developing the paravesical spaces, identifies and sections the vasa deferentia, and carefully identifies and preserves the endopelvic fascia.
The surgeon prepares to remove the lateral pelvic lymph nodes following the completion of the extirpative phase, with visible attention to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies, dissects, and sections the bladder pedicles in an undergrade fashion, aligning the dissection plane with the previously developed posterior plane during prostatic dissection.

The surgeon lifts the bladder and uses low-energy bipolar cautery to perform interfacial dissection, partly preserving the neurovascular bundles. The dissection proceeds in an antegrade fashion until reaching the puboprostatic ligaments. The surgical field shows some bleeding due to perforating vessels from the neurovascular bundles to the prostate. Robotic arms maintain tissue under traction to identify the proper dissection plane.
The surgeon frees the anterior surface of the bladder up to the puboprostatic ligaments, ensuring the preservation of the puboprostatic ligaments during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies and sections the Dorsal Venous Complex (DBC) or Santorini plexus, achieving hemostasis with a running suture.
The surgeon dissects and sections the urethra at the prostatic apex. The Foley catheter provides traction on the posterior surface of the urethra and the fibers of the recto-urethralis muscle.
The surgeon places the excised surgical specimen into an endobag for removal from the surgical field.
The screen displays the prostatic ligaments highlighted in orange, the pelvic aponeurosis in yellow, and the proximal part of the preserved neurovascular bundles in blue.

The neobladder technique that we have implemented combines the advantages of several other techniques. A good volume, as in Hautmann's neobladder, a reduced number of continuous sutures, as in Wiklund's neobladder, yet without the difficulty of performing horizontal sutures, such as in the Fox Z neobladder.

The surgeon identifies the site for the urethra neovascular anastomosis approximately twenty centimeters from the ileocecal valve. The bowel is anchored to the perirethral tissue, and an incision is made to define the anastomotic rim.
The surgeon begins the urethra neovascular anastomosis using a barbed suture. A new catheter is inserted into the urethra before the suture is closed.
The surgeon uses a periscopic stapler to isolate a section of the bowel, visually separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to carefully remove the staple lines from the bowel, revealing the underlying tissue.
The surgeon performs a lateral-lateral anastomosis in the bowel using a laparoscopic stapler, aligning the bowel segments for stapling.
The surgeon performs tubularization of the bowel using monopolar cautery on the anti-mesenteric aspects, with gentle retraction facilitated by the surgical arms.
The surgeon aligns the two newly identified posterior segments of the bowel, ensuring proper orientation for subsequent surgical steps.
The surgeon performs a posterior continuous suture as the initial step, securing the aligned posterior segments of the bowel.
The surgeon de-tubularizes the remaining ileal segment, followed by performing a second continuous posterior suture on the bowel.

The surgeon performs an anterior continuous suture to close the neobladder. Ureteral catheters are visibly passed through the abdominal wall, inserted through the upper wall of the neobladder, and placed into the ureters.

The surgeon fixes the urethral catheters to the urethra using a transfixion suture. A direct anastomosis is then performed with interrupted stitches. The upper portion of the neobladder is closed with a final continuous suture, followed by a leak test to ensure integrity.
